desc: Milbert's robber fly — one of the largest robber flies in LA County (30mm+). Catches prey in mid-air including dragonflies, grasshoppers, and large bees. Bristly face with mystax (mustache) of stiff hairs protects eyes during prey capture. Powerful predator. Impressive.

fm.Behavior: Aerial predator; catches prey in flight; perches on prominent twigs
fm.Diet: Predator — dragonflies, grasshoppers, bees, other large insects

hp: Predator of grasshoppers — important rangeland biocontrol. Preys on grasshopper. Predatory.

desc: Red-legged giant robber fly — one of the largest robber flies in NA (25-30mm). Bright orange-red legs diagnostic. An apex aerial predator — catches other robber flies, dragonflies, and large bees in midair. Perches on tips of vegetation scanning for prey. Impressive and intimidating.

fm.Behavior: Aggressive aerial predator; sits on fence posts, bare branches
fm.Diet: Predator — other robber flies, bees, large insects

hp: Aerial predator of flying insects.

desc: Large bee fly — fuzzy bombyliid that feeds at flowers while hovering, using its remarkably long rigid proboscis. Resembles a small bumblebee. Larvae parasitize solitary ground-nesting bees — female flicks eggs toward bee burrow entrance in flight. Common spring visitor to gardens.

fm.Color: Fuzzy golden-brown; long straight proboscis; dark wing front edge
fm.Behavior: Hovers at flowers; flicks eggs into bee burrows in flight
fm.Habitat: Gardens, chaparral, meadows — near ground-nesting bees
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize ground-nesting bee larvae

hp: Densely hairy.

desc: Large, spectacular bombyliid with dramatically mottled tiger-pattern wings. Parasitizes carpenter bees (Xylocopa) — female lays eggs near carpenter bee nest entrance. Larva enters nest and consumes bee larva. One of the most photogenic flies in LA County.

fm.Behavior: Hovers near carpenter bee nests; lays eggs in nest holes
fm.Habitat: Near wooden structures with carpenter bee holes
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize carpenter bee larvae

hp: Bee fly — larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees. Parasitoid of Xylocopa (carpenter bees). Bombyliidae.

desc: Sinuous bee fly — dark bee fly with wavy dark wing markings. Larvae parasitize tachinid flies (hyperparasitoid). Common in chaparral. Hovers at flowers.
hp: Bee fly — larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees. Parasitoid. Dark bee fly with wavy dark wing markings. Larvae parasitize.

desc: Western giant crane fly — the largest fly in North America with wingspan exceeding 3 inches. Completely harmless despite terrifying mosquito-like appearance — cannot bite or sting. Adults live only a few days, do not feed. Larvae in moist soil and streams. A familiar SoCal sight at porch lights.

fm.Color: Brown; very long legs; mosquito-like but MUCH larger
fm.Behavior: Attracted to lights; clumsy flight; adults do not feed; completely harmless
fm.Habitat: Near streams, moist areas — adults at lights
fm.Diet: Adults: do not feed (mouthparts non-functional). Larvae: aquatic detritus

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Tipulidae.

desc: Large (20mm) biting tabanid. Females deliver painful slashing bites — blade-like mouthparts cut skin, then lap blood. Near livestock and water where larvae develop in wet soil. Males feed only on nectar. Among the largest biting flies in LA County.

fm.Behavior: Females bite — slash skin, lap blood; painful; males visit flowers
fm.Diet: Females: blood. Males: nectar, pollen

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Tabanidae.

desc: Striking parasitoid fly with bright orange abdomen and distinctive feathery fringe on hind tibiae (diagnostic — no other NA fly has this). Parasitizes stink bugs and squash bugs — lays egg on host. Important beneficial fly in vegetable gardens.

fm.Color: Black thorax; bright orange abdomen; feathered hind tibiae
fm.Behavior: Parasitizes squash bugs and stink bugs; lays eggs on host
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize Hemiptera (beneficial)

hp: Important garden biocontrol. Associated with Arizona Ash.

desc: Metallic green-gold. First insect to colonize carrion — important in forensic entomology. Larvae used medically for wound cleaning.

fm.Behavior: First to arrive at carrion; important forensic indicator
fm.Habitat: Ubiquitous — attracted to carrion, garbage, wounds
fm.Diet: Larvae: carrion, decaying tissue

hp: On carrion — essential decomposer. Forensically important. Metallic coloring.

desc: Large metallic blue blow fly with distinctive loud buzzing flight. Around garbage, carrion, and pet waste. Forensically important — arrival time on corpses helps determine time of death. Despite unpleasant habits, performs essential decomposition services.

fm.Behavior: Loud buzzer; attracted to meat, garbage
fm.Diet: Larvae: carrion, waste

hp: Necrophagous — on carrion. Essential decomposer. Forensically important.

desc: Looks nearly identical to a house fly but BITES. Forward-pointing bayonet-like proboscis pierces skin of humans and livestock. Blood-feeder. Breeds in decaying vegetation and animal waste, not dung (unlike horn flies). Significant livestock pest causing weight loss.

fm.Color: Gray; like house fly but with bayonet-like proboscis (forward-pointing)
fm.Behavior: Painful biter; bites legs; rests on walls with head up; blood feeder
fm.Habitat: Near livestock, stables, dog runs — organic waste
fm.Diet: Blood — mammals including humans

hp: On dung — nutrient recycler and parasite control. Ant associate.

desc: Ethereal crane fly that floats through shaded woodland like a ghost. Inflated tarsi (foot segments) act as tiny parachutes or pontoons, enabling buoyant, slow-motion flight. One of the most remarkable and beautiful flies in LA's riparian habitats. Harmless.

fm.Size: Body ⅓ in–½ in; legs very long with inflated tarsi
fm.Color: Black and white banded legs; inflated white tarsal segments
fm.Behavior: Drifts slowly through shade; inflated feet act as parachutes; ethereal
fm.Diet: Adults: do not feed. Larvae: aquatic

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Riparian habitat.

desc: Orange-banded mydas fly — one of the largest flies in North America (30mm+). Convincing wasp mimic with orange abdominal band on black body. Impressive hovering flight. Larvae predatory in rotting wood. Adults visit flowers. The large size and wasp mimicry are unmistakable.

fm.Color: Black with orange second abdominal segment — wasp mimic
fm.Behavior: Powerful hoverer; visits flowers; Batesian wasp mimic
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: in rotting wood/soil (predatory)

hp: Predatory — natural pest control. Mimicry specialist.

desc: Larvae are champion composters — process organic waste rapidly. Commercially raised for waste management and animal feed.

fm.Color: Black; wasp-like; two translucent 'windows' on abdomen
fm.Behavior: Adults harmless; larvae consume organic waste; commercial composting
fm.Diet: Larvae: decomposing organic matter (champion composters)

hp: Black soldier fly — larvae process organic waste 10x composting rate. Commercially farmed as protein source (Sheppard et al. 1994).

desc: Mediterranean fruit fly — dreaded quarantine pest. NOT the tiny Drosophila vinegar fly — medfly is a larger tephritid whose larvae destroy 250+ fruit and vegetable crops. Periodic SoCal outbreaks trigger quarantine zones with fruit movement restrictions. CDFA actively monitors with traps.

fm.Color: Yellow-brown; patterned wings; large for a fruit fly
fm.Behavior: Lays eggs in ripening fruit; causes massive agricultural damage
fm.Diet: Larvae: ripening fruit — citrus, stone fruit, many others

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Tephritidae.

desc: Invasive from Asia. Unlike other vinegar flies, attacks RIPE (not rotting) fruit. Males have dark wing spots. Major berry pest.

fm.Color: Tan; ♂ has dark spot on each wing (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Lays eggs in intact ripe fruit; devastating berry/cherry pest
fm.Habitat: Berry farms, orchards, gardens with fruit
fm.Diet: Larvae: ripe fruit — berries, cherries, grapes

hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling. Invasive.

desc: Giant, iridescent mosquito that does NOT bite humans. Larvae are predatory — eat other mosquito larvae. Beneficial.

fm.Size: ½ in–⅔ in (giant for a mosquito)
fm.Color: Metallic blue-green iridescence; curved proboscis (can't pierce skin)
fm.Behavior: Hovers near vegetation; does NOT bite; larvae eat mosquito larvae
fm.Habitat: Tree holes, containers — wherever standing water collects
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar only. Larvae: predatory on mosquito larvae (beneficial)

hp: Predator of mosquito larvae — natural mosquito biocontrol. Preys on mosquito.

desc: Gray, sluggish fly that clusters in buildings during fall and winter by the hundreds. Larvae are internal parasites of earthworms — not a filth fly (does not breed in garbage). Often confused with house flies but larger, slower, and with golden thoracic hairs.

fm.Color: Dark gray; golden hairs on thorax; sluggish
fm.Behavior: Mass clusters in buildings fall/winter; sluggish; not attracted to garbage
fm.Diet: Adults: nectar. Larvae: parasitize earthworms

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Parasitoid. Large species.

desc: Attractive small fly with ornately banded/patterned wings. Waves wings slowly in display on leaf surfaces. On decaying vegetation. The wing-waving behavior is thought to be a visual signal, possibly mimicking jumping spiders to deter real spiders.

fm.Color: Dark with patterned/banded wings; wing-waving behavior
fm.Behavior: Elaborate wing-waving display; on decaying plants
fm.Diet: Larvae: decaying plant material

hp: On decaying vegetation. Wing-waving display may mimic jumping spiders. Batesian mimicry hypothesis.

desc: Small gray fly whose larvae are the 'root maggots' that damage Brassicaceae crops. Important agricultural pest.

fm.Color: Gray; similar to small house fly
fm.Behavior: Lays eggs at base of plants; larvae bore into roots
fm.Diet: Larvae: roots of Brassicaceae (major pest)

hp: Root maggot — larvae destroy Brassicaceae crop roots (cabbage, broccoli, radish). Major agricultural pest.

desc: White-spotted drain fly — tiny (3mm) fuzzy moth-like psychodid found in bathrooms. Breeds in drain biofilm — the organic slime lining shower drains and sink pipes. Also called drain moth or sewer gnat. Hairy wings held roof-like. Indicator of drain cleaning needs. Harmless.

fm.Behavior: Sits on bathroom walls; breeds in drain biofilm; poor flier
fm.Diet: Larvae: biofilm in drains and sewage

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Psychodidae.

desc: Tiny wingless fly that rides on honeybees. Not a true louse. Steals food from bee's mouth. Declining with bee declines.

fm.Color: Brown; round; wingless; resembles a mite
fm.Behavior: Rides on adult honeybees; steals food from bee's mouth (kleptoparasite)
fm.Diet: Kleptoparasite — steals food from honeybees

hp: Wingless fly riding on honeybees. Steals food from bee's mouthparts. Not a true parasite.

desc: Metallic green fly. Forensically important — one of first insects to colonize remains. Also causes myiasis in sheep.
hp: Forensically important — arrival time on remains used to estimate time of death.

desc: Tiny (1.5mm) biting ceratopogonid causing seasonal misery in inland valleys. Breeds in moist alkaline soil. Swarms at dawn and dusk. Bite is painful relative to the midge's minute size. Historically notorious in San Fernando and San Gabriel Valleys.

fm.Size: Tiny biting midge causing seasonal misery in inland valleys

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ceratopogonidae.

desc: Coffin fly — tiny (2mm) phorid fly that runs rapidly rather than flying (characteristic bouncing run diagnostic). Breeds in an extraordinary range of decaying organic matter, including buried remains. Forensic indicator species. Can bore through burial soil. Cosmopolitan.

fm.Size: Tiny fly that runs rapidly rather than flying

hp: Wood/stem borer. Phoridae.

desc: Pigeon louse fly — flat, wingless hippoboscid fly parasitizing pigeons and doves. Crab-like body scuttles through feathers. Blood-feeder. Vectors Haemoproteus (avian malaria parasite). Can temporarily bite humans when pigeons nest on buildings. Called 'pigeon fly' or 'flat fly.'
hp: Obligate ectoparasite — blood feeder on specific host.

desc: Drain fly — tiny (2mm) fuzzy moth-like fly breeding in biofilm buildup in sink and shower drains. Hairy wings held roof-like over body. Indicator of drain maintenance needs — presence means organic buildup in pipes. Harmless but annoying. Also called moth fly.

fm.Size: Tiny fuzzy fly breeding in sink and shower drains

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Psychodidae.

desc: Small dark fly frequently seen on windows. Larvae in fermenting organic matter — tree sap, compost, fungal growth. Sometimes called 'wood gnats.' Part of the small family Anisopodidae. Common indoors near windows. Harmless. Attracted to light.
hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. In compost.

desc: Benedict's robber fly — large bristly-faced asilid ambushing insects in flight. Powerful predator of bees, wasps, and other large insects. Massive thoracic flight muscles power explosive attack launches from perch. Named for entomologist Benedict. Chaparral and open habitats.

fm.Prey: Bees, wasps, other flies — caught in flight

hp: Generalist insect predator. Chaparral habitat. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Sordid deer fly — biting tabanid with banded wings and brilliant iridescent green eyes. Females require blood meals for egg development — deliver slashing, painful bites. Near water where larvae develop in wet soil. Males feed on nectar only. Distinguished by wing bands.
hp: Female blood-feeder. Can transmit tularemia.

desc: California archytas — large bristly tachinid parasitizing caterpillars. Important natural biocontrol of armyworms and cutworms in agriculture. Female deposits eggs on foliage; caterpillar ingests egg while feeding; larva develops inside host. One of the largest tachinids in LA.
hp: Biocontrol agent; parasitoid of moths. Preys on caterpillar. Large bristly tachinid parasitizing caterpillars.

desc: Red-tailed flesh fly — gray-striped fly depositing live larvae (not eggs) directly on carrion. Three dark thoracic stripes diagnostic. Forensically important — larviposition timing used in death investigations. Distinguished from blow flies by depositing live maggots.

fm.Color: Gray with three dark thoracic stripes
fm.Habitat: Carrion; also parasitic on other insects
fm.Behavior: Deposits live larvae not eggs (larviparous)

hp: On carrion — essential decomposer. Forensically important. Ant associate.

desc: Siphon long-legged fly — tiny metallic green dolichopodid with elaborate male courtship displays. Males wave ornate, flag-like tarsal decorations during mating dances. Predatory on tiny insects on leaf surfaces. The metallic green and courtship flags are visible with a hand lens.

fm.Behavior: ♂ waves ornate leg flags in courtship display

hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Metallic coloring.

desc: Small (4mm) black fly found indoors on windows. Adults harmless. Larvae are the real story — they live in carpets and rugs where they prey on carpet beetle larvae, providing natural biological control of textile pests. Entirely beneficial household insect.

fm.Habitat: Small black fly found indoors on windows

hp: Introduced for biological pest control. Scenopinidae.

desc: Moustachioed snipe fly — medium predatory fly that perches head-down on tree trunks and fence posts, pouncing on passing insects. Pointed, elongated head. Named for the distinctive facial hair tuft. Larvae predatory in soil and leaf litter. Woodland and garden edges.

fm.Size: Predatory fly perching head-down on tree trunks

hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Van Duzee's kelp fly — small fly breeding in enormous numbers in rotting kelp wrack on beaches. Essential decomposer — processes tons of stranded seaweed. Huge swarms can form over wrack lines. Named for hemipterist E.P. Van Duzee who also collected flies.

fm.Habitat: Small fly breeding in rotting kelp on beaches

hp: Beach wrack decomposer — breaks down stranded kelp and seaweed. Coastal nutrient cycling. Beach and dune habitat.

desc: White-winged march fly — black bibionid swarming in spring. Males with enormous compound eyes (nearly meeting on top of head). Larvae feed on decomposing roots and organic matter in soil. The mating swarms can be dense and conspicuous in March-April (hence 'march flies').
hp: Detritivore — breaks down organic matter. Soil-dwelling. Decomposer.

desc: Forked thick-headed fly — wasp-mimicking conopid. Internal parasitoid of bumblebees — female intercepts foraging bee in flight, inserts egg between abdominal segments. Larva develops inside living bee, eventually killing it. Devastating to individual bees but natural population regulator.
hp: Parasitoid — biological control agent. Thick-headed.

desc: The world's most widespread and familiar fly. Disease vector — mechanically transmits pathogens from filth to food via body hairs and mouthpart regurgitation. Complex compound eyes with ~4,000 facets. Breeds in organic waste. 12 generations per year in LA's warm climate.

fm.Color: Gray with four dark thoracic stripes

hp: Mechanical disease vector — carries pathogens from filth to food. Breeds in organic waste. Prey for spiders, swallows, and robber flies.

desc: Tiny vinegar fly on ripening fruit. The most studied multicellular organism in the history of biology — six Nobel Prizes have been won using this species. Revolutionary for understanding genetics, development, neuroscience, and aging. 75% of human disease genes have fly orthologs.

fm.Role: Most important model organism in genetics

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Drosophilidae.

desc: Giant robber fly — one of the largest flies in North America (35mm+). Ambushes large insects including dragonflies, other robber flies, and carpenter bees in midair. Immensely powerful thoracic flight muscles. Perches on exposed branches scanning for prey. Breathtaking predator.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Large species.

desc: Tiny fly parasitizing fire ants. Lays egg in ant — larva decapitates host. Released as biocontrol of fire ants.
hp: Fire ant decapitating fly — parasitoid whose larva migrates to ant's head, causing decapitation. Introduced for fire ant biocontrol.

desc: Large (20mm) dark bee-mimic fly. Larvae are subcutaneous parasites developing under the skin of rodents and rabbits — forming a large cyst with breathing hole. Occasionally infests outdoor cats and dogs. Adult short-lived, non-feeding. Multiple species in LA County.

fm.Role: Parasitoid — larvae develop under host skin

hp: Mimicry — Batesian or aggressive. Mimicry specialist. Large species.

desc: Small tephritid with distinctive banded wings. Larvae bore into and destroy cherries and other stone fruit. Important pest of cherry orchards in the West. Pupae overwinter in soil. Female ovipositor pierces cherry skin to lay eggs.
hp: Larvae develop inside fruit. Crop pest. Associated with Cherry.

desc: Small tephritid with banded wings whose larvae stain and destroy walnut husks. Common wherever English and black walnuts grow in LA. Pupae overwinter in soil. Major pest causing premature nut drop and kernel shrivel. Emerged as a California pest in the 1920s.
hp: On walnut (juglans) in riparian/foothill habitat. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Small tephritid with distinctive F-shaped wing pattern. Quarantine pest — not established in California, actively monitored by CDFA with trapping programs. If established, would devastate apple, cherry, and pear crops. Any detection triggers emergency response.

fm.Habitat: Apple orchards — quarantine pest in CA

hp: Quarantine pest — larvae in apple, cherry, pear. Not established in CA. Active CDFA monitoring.

desc: Devastating tephritid pest of olives from the Mediterranean. Arrived in California in 1998 and rapidly colonized all olive-growing areas. Larvae tunnel through olive flesh, ruining fruit for both table and oil use. Major economic impact on SoCal olive industry.

fm.Habitat: Olive trees — established in CA since 1998

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Tephritidae.

desc: Invasive ankle-biter established in LA County since 2011. Day-biting. Vectors dengue, Zika, chikungunya — first locally acquired dengue in LA history 2024.

fm.Size: ¼ in; black with white leg bands
fm.Habitat: Urban — breeds in any tiny water container
fm.Behavior: Aggressive daytime biter
fm.Disease: Vectors dengue, Zika, chikungunya, yellow fever

hp: Introduced species. Invasive.

desc: Invasive black-and-white striped mosquito. Aggressive daytime biter. Established in parts of LA County. Vectors dengue and chikungunya.

fm.Size: ¼ in; black with single white dorsal stripe
fm.Habitat: Urban — any small container with standing water

hp: Blood-feeding mosquito — disease vector. Potential disease vector. Preys on mosquito.

desc: LA's most common night-biting mosquito. Primary vector of West Nile virus in Southern California. Also vectors St. Louis encephalitis and dog heartworm. Breeds in standing water — storm drains, birdbaths, neglected pools. Year-round in LA's mild climate.

fm.Habitat: Storm drains, pools, any stagnant water
fm.Disease: Primary West Nile virus vector in SoCal

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Mosquito associate.

desc: Invasive Old World blow fly — most abundant blow fly in LA County. Metallic blue-green. Predatory larvae. Key forensic entomology species.
hp: Invasive introduction — ecological impact on native communities.

desc: Invasive metallic blue-green blow fly from the Old World. Large-headed (megacephala). Forensically important — timing of colonization on remains used in death investigations. Dark anterior spiracle distinguishes from native Calliphora. Increasingly common in LA.
hp: Introduced species. Ant associate.

desc: Tiny humpbacked biting fly breeding in flowing water. Target of LA River vector control programs. Females slash skin.

fm.Behavior: Females bite painfully; huge swarms near water

hp: Blood-feeding ectoparasite. Potential disease vector.

desc: Tiny agromyzid whose larvae mine serpentine (winding) trails through leaf tissue, creating characteristic white meandering lines. Major greenhouse and agricultural pest of lettuce, celery, peppers. Develops insecticide resistance. Larvae between leaf surfaces.

fm.Damage: Larvae mine visible serpentine trails in leaves

hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Agromyzidae. Very small species. Fly. Liriomyza.

desc: Red-legged kelp fly — small dark anthomyiid breeding in decomposing kelp and seaweed on LA County beaches. Essential coastal decomposer — breaks down beach wrack, recycling marine nutrients into the terrestrial ecosystem. Can form large swarms over kelp piles. Native.

fm.Habitat: Sandy beaches — decomposing kelp wrack line

hp: Coastal nutrient cycling.

desc: The 'mosquito-like' insect that doesn't bite. Enormous swarms near water at dusk. Males have feathery antennae. Larvae ('bloodworms') are bright red.

fm.Behavior: Huge dusk swarms; does NOT bite

hp: Blood-feeding mosquito. Breeds in standing water. Disease vector.

desc: Large introduced crane fly whose larvae ('leatherjackets') damage turf by feeding on grass roots. Adults are the alarming 'giant mosquitoes' at porch lights — completely harmless, cannot bite or sting. One of the most commonly misidentified insects.

fm.Size: ½–⅔ in body; very long legs
fm.Behavior: Cannot bite or sting; clumsy flier

hp: Crane fly — harmless despite alarming size. Larvae in soil/water. Not a mosquito. Preys on mosquito.

desc: The small fly that hovers in lazy triangular patterns under ceiling lights. Smaller and more slender than house fly.

fm.Size: ⅛–¼ in; smaller than house fly
fm.Behavior: Males hover in lazy triangles under lights

hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. Fanniidae.

desc: Tiny dark fly swarming around houseplant soil. Larvae feed on fungi and roots in potting mix. #1 indoor plant pest fly.
hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Tiny fly that induces distinctive round bud galls on coyote bush. Galls far more conspicuous than the 1mm adults.

fm.Gall: Round bud galls on Baccharis — very conspicuous

hp: Plant gall-inducer. Each species forms unique gall structures.

desc: Large gray fly with three black thoracic stripes and checkered abdomen. Deposits live larvae (not eggs) on carrion and excrement. Common urban fly. Forensic indicator.

fm.Color: Gray with 3 black thoracic stripes; checkered abdomen
fm.Behavior: Deposits live larvae (larviparity)

hp: On carrion — essential decomposer. Forensically important. Urban habitat.

desc: Slender tachinid with orange-red head and narrow waist. Parasitizes true bugs (Pentatomidae). A beneficial insect that helps control stink bugs.

fm.Color: Orange-red head; black body; narrow waist

hp: Parasitoid of insect hosts. Parasitoid of stink bugs (Pentatomidae).

desc: Fuzzy bee mimic with long fixed proboscis. Hovers at flowers. Larvae are parasitoids of solitary bee nests. Flicks eggs into bee nest entrances while hovering.

fm.Behavior: Flicks eggs into solitary bee nests while hovering

hp: Parasitoid of solitary bee/wasp nests. Bee mimic. Densely hairy.

desc: Large flat wasp-mimic fly with yellow abdominal markings. Larvae aquatic in stagnant water. Adults visit flowers. Important pollinator.

fm.Color: Black with yellow abdominal spots; flat body
fm.Habitat: Near water — larvae aquatic; adults on flowers

hp: Spotted soldier fly — adult visits flowers; larvae aquatic with calcareous cuticle. Near stagnant water. Part of aquatic food web.

desc: Tiny brilliant metallic green fly on leaf surfaces. Males perform elaborate courtship displays waving modified tarsi. Predatory on tiny insects. Very common in gardens.

fm.Habitat: Leaf surfaces in gardens — tiny predator
fm.Behavior: ♂ elaborate courtship with modified feet

hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens. Metallic coloring.

desc: Wasp-mimic fly with enlarged head. Parasitoid — female intercepts bumblebees in flight, inserts egg into abdomen. Larva develops inside living bee. Remarkable biology.

fm.Color: Black and yellow wasp mimic; enlarged head
fm.Behavior: Parasitizes living bumblebees — injects egg in flight

hp: Parasitoid of solitary bee/wasp nests. Bee mimic.

desc: Similar to house fly but eyes closer together (♀) or touching (♂). Feeds on eye and nose secretions of livestock. Enters houses in fall seeking overwintering sites.

fm.Color: Gray; eyes closer together than house fly
fm.Habitat: Near livestock — feeds on facial secretions
fm.Behavior: Invades homes in fall for winter shelter

hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. Muscidae.

desc: Small fly with striking dark-patterned wings. Waves wings in semaphore-like courtship display on leaves. Breeds in decaying vegetation. Common on compost.

fm.Behavior: Waves wings in semaphore display on leaf surfaces

hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling. In compost. Ulidiidae.

desc: Small dark fly of alkaline lakes and saline pools. Larvae feed on algae in mineral-rich water. Important food source for shorebirds at Mono Lake (and similar LA County playas).
hp: Leaf miner — larvae in leaf tissue. Near water. Important prey for insectivorous birds.

desc: Tiny fly attracted to eyes and wounds. Persistent and annoying. Can transmit pinkeye. The classic 'annoying SoCal gnat.'
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Chloropidae.

desc: Extraordinary fly that mimics ichneumon wasps — walks on front 4 legs, waves black-tipped forelegs like antennae. Remarkable behavioral mimicry.

fm.Behavior: Waves forelegs like wasp antennae — behavioral mimic

hp: Ant mimic — may prey on ants. Wasp mimic.

desc: Elongated yellow-and-black soldier fly. Larvae in compost. Adults visit flowers. Not related to BSF but same family.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling. In compost. Larvae in compost.

desc: Scorched tachinid — large bristly fly whose larvae parasitize cutworm and armyworm caterpillars. Important agricultural biological control agent — a single female can parasitize dozens of pest caterpillars. Named for the charred (adusta) coloring of the thorax.
hp: Biocontrol agent; parasitoid of moths. Preys on caterpillar. Large bristly fly whose larvae parasitize cutworm and armywo.

desc: Golden-haired fly on vegetation. Predatory. Often hangs from leaves head-down waiting to pounce on passing insects.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Snipe.

desc: Tiny shiny black fly that waves its wings constantly while walking. Common on dung and compost. Distinctive wing-waving courtship display.

fm.Behavior: Constantly waves wings while walking

hp: On dung — nutrient recycler and parasite control. In compost.

desc: Tiny dark fly breeding in fermenting organic matter, drains, and compost. Very common indoors. Often confused with fungus gnats.
hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. In compost.

desc: Transparent aquatic larvae ('glass worms') in ponds — nearly invisible predators. Adults at lights near water. Related to mosquitoes but don't bite.
hp: Phantom midge — transparent aquatic larvae ("glass worms") prey on zooplankton.

desc: Spectacular bumblebee mimic with dense golden-brown pile. Aggressive aerial predator catching insects in flight. Very common in SoCal gardens and scrubland.

fm.Behavior: Catches prey in flight; perches on exposed twigs

hp: Generalist insect predator. Beneficial in gardens. Bee mimic.

desc: Brilliant metallic green long-legged fly. Males perform elaborate wing-waving courtship on sunlit leaves. One of the most commonly photographed tiny flies. Predatory on mites and gnats.

fm.Behavior: ♂ wing-waving courtship on sunlit leaves

hp: Metallic long-legged fly — fast predator of tiny insects on leaf surfaces.

desc: Small syrphid with four dark wing spots. Very common at flowers. Larvae prey on aphids. One of the most abundant hover flies in western gardens.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. In gardens

desc: Distinctive stilt-legged fly with patterned wings that waves its front legs like antennae. Mimics parasitoid wasps. Found on decaying vegetation.

fm.Behavior: Waves front legs like wasp antennae

hp: Ant mimic — body shape, gait, and coloring mimic ants. Batesian mimicry. Wasp mimic.

desc: Medium syrphid with distinctive white comma marks on dark abdomen. Strong flier — can hover motionlessly. Larvae devour aphids.

fm.Color: Dark with white comma marks on abdomen

hp: Builds horizontal sheet/dome web. Preys on aphids. Aphid predator.

desc: Yellow-and-black patterned. Despite fearsome appearance, completely harmless. Larvae in soil. Very common at lights on warm evenings.

desc: Tiny cecidomyiid inducing distinctive grooved (sulcate) stem galls on California sagebrush (Artemisia californica). Each gall houses a single larva. Very common but overlooked. Part of the enormous Rhopalomyia radiation on sagebrush — multiple species each induce species-specific gall forms.
hp: Gall-inducer on host plant

desc: Medium metallic green dolichopodid found on wet rocks near streams. Predatory — catches tiny insects on rock surfaces. The metallic green sheen is vivid. Part of the diverse stream-margin dolichopodid community. Very fast movements.
hp: Predator near streams — hunts small insects.

desc: Small dark mycetophilid. Larvae on fungi. Part of the diverse but overlooked fungus gnat community.
hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi and fungal hyphae. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Mycetophilidae.

desc: Remarkable chironomid with FULLY MARINE larvae living in rocky intertidal zone. One of very few marine insects. Adults tiny, short-lived.

fm.Note: Marine intertidal larvae — among few truly marine insects

hp: Rocky intertidal — between high and low tide marks on LA County shores. Spiny/armed.

desc: Medium hover fly with skull-like yellow mark on thorax. Common at flowers. Larvae in tree holes and stagnant water.

fm.Color: Dark with skull-like yellow thorax mark

hp: Hover fly — adult visits flowers; larvae variable (aphid predator or decomposer). Ant associate.

desc: Medium tachinid with feathered hind legs. Parasitizes stink bugs. Important biocontrol agent of crop-damaging pentatomids.

fm.Color: Dark; feathered hind legs; parasitizes stink bugs

hp: Biocontrol agent; parasitoid. Parasitoid of stink bugs (Pentatomidae).

desc: Large (30mm) mydas fly with leopard-spotted pattern. Among the largest flies in LA County. Powerful flight — impressive hovering ability. Larvae predatory in soil. Adults visit flowers. The spotted pattern and large size make identification immediate.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Predatory. Large species.

desc: Medium golden-yellow horse fly with green eyes. Female delivers scissor-like bite. Less aggressive than Chrysops.
hp: Giant silvius — large horse fly of foothill chaparral. Female blood-feeder with painful bite. Near water and livestock.

desc: Small dark simuliid of mountain streams. Female bites. Larvae filter-feed attached to rocks in fast water. Important stream organism.
hp: Stream and river habitats. Aquatic or semi-aquatic lifestyle. Ant associate.

desc: Small predatory empidid. Males present prey items to females as nuptial gifts during mating — a famous example of sexual selection. In swarms near water.
hp: Predator — hunts other insects. Important in natural pest control. Predatory.

desc: Very large fly with orange/yellow wings. One of the largest flies. Bee mimic. Impressive.

fm.Color: Dark body; yellow-orange wings; bee mimic

hp: Wasp-mimicking mydas fly — Batesian mimicry with orange wings. Larvae predatory in soil.

desc: Medium bee fly with spotted wings. Larvae parasitize solitary bee and wasp nests. Common at flowers.
hp: Bee fly — larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees. Solitary. Family Bombyliidae. Medium bee fly with spotted wings. Larvae parasitize solitar.

desc: Medium bombyliid with diagnostic divided/split dark wing pattern. Hovers near ground seeking host burrows. Larvae parasitize moth pupae or ground-nesting bees. At flowers in desert and foothill habitats. The wing pattern distinguishes from similar species.
hp: Burrowing ambush predator. Desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: Medium dark-winged bombyliid. Larvae parasitize moth pupae or ground-nesting insects. Adults at flowers on sandy ground.
hp: Family Bombyliidae.

desc: Two-spotted soldier fly — small green stratiomyid with two diagnostic dark thoracic spots. Near standing water. Larvae aquatic with distinctive calcareous (calcium-rich) cuticle. Adults visit flowers. One of several green soldier flies near LA waterways.
hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Family Stratiomyidae. Larvae aquatic with distinctive calcareous (calcium-rich) cuticle.

desc: Small metallic green dolichopodid. Predatory on tiny insects on leaves. Fast runner on foliage.
hp: Generalist predator of insects and other arthropods. Predatory. Metallic coloring.

desc: Medium metallic green stratiomyid. Common at flowers. Larvae in soil and compost. Attractive metallic coloring.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling. Stratiomyidae.

desc: Tiny dark stratiomyid. Under bark. Larvae in decaying wood. Very small for a soldier fly.
hp: On tree bark. Part of bark-dwelling community. Under bark. Very small species.

desc: Medium gray muscid resembling house fly. In stables, barns, and around livestock. Larvae in decaying organic matter. Can spread disease mechanically.
hp: Found in and around buildings and structures. Muscidae.

desc: Small dark fly breeding in cheese, cured meats, and stored animal products. Larvae can jump several inches ('skipping'). Famous pest of aging cheese.

fm.Behavior: Larvae skip/jump several inches

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Piophilidae.

desc: Extremely common fruit fly, nearly identical to D. melanogaster. Major genetics model organism. On rotting fruit worldwide.

fm.Color: Tan; virtually identical to common fruit fly

hp: Detritivore — breaks down dead organic matter. Important in nutrient cycling. Drosophilidae.

desc: Tiny (2mm) sphaerocerid with diagnostic reddish antennae. On dung, compost, and decaying organic matter. Part of the enormous family Sphaeroceridae — tiny flies that decompose organic waste. Ecologically important despite being nearly invisible to the unaided eye.
hp: Saproxylic — in dead wood. Important in forest nutrient cycling and decomposition. On dung.

desc: Small fly breeding in decaying seaweed on beaches. Very common on wrack lines. Important beach decomposer.
hp: Beach wrack decomposer — breaks down stranded kelp and seaweed. Coastal nutrient cycling. Beach and dune habitat.

desc: Rayed picture-wing fly — small tephritid with radiating dark wing pattern resembling tiny spokes. On composites (Asteraceae). Larvae develop inside flower heads. The wing pattern may serve as a visual signal during mating displays. Common on native and weedy composites.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Tephritidae.

desc: Small dark empidid. Predatory. Part of the dance fly family known for male nuptial prey gifts.
hp: Predator — hunts other insects. Important in natural pest control. Predatory.

desc: Tiny fly inducing leaf-margin roll galls on black locust (Robinia). Introduced from Europe. Very common on street tree locusts.

fm.Color: Tiny; rolled leaf margins on locust

hp: Plant gall-inducer. Each species forms unique gall structures. Tiny. Tiny fly inducing leaf-margin roll galls on black locust (Ro.

desc: Small dark mycetophilid whose larvae feed on bracket fungi and mushrooms in woodland. Adults at lights near woodland. Named 'varia' for variable body coloring. Part of the enormous but poorly studied fungus gnat diversity.
hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi and fungal hyphae. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Medium bristly parasitoid fly. Named for Dutch dipterist Friedrich M. van der Wulp (1818-1899). Larvae develop inside caterpillar hosts. Part of the enormous Tachinidae — over 8,000 species worldwide, all parasitoids. Important natural pest control agents.
hp: Predator or parasitoid of caterpillars — natural pest control. Preys on caterpillar. Caterpillar predator.

desc: Medium bee fly with three-toothed wing cells. Desert and foothill habitats. At flowers.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Desert habitat. Family Bombyliidae. Three-toothed.

desc: Small anisopodid found at windows. Named 'cinctus' (banded). Larvae in fermenting organic matter, tree sap flows, and decaying wood. Adults attracted to light. Part of the small family Anisopodidae — window gnats, among the more primitive Diptera.

fm.Color: Dark with pale bands; at windows

hp: Flower-visiting flies — ecological role from description context. Anisopodidae.

desc: Plantain leafminer — tiny agromyzid creating white serpentine mines in plantain (Plantago) leaves. Each mine contains a single larva visible as a dark speck through the translucent leaf. Very common on plantain in lawns and gardens. Part of the enormous Phytomyza genus.
hp: Leaf miner — larvae tunnel between leaf surfaces creating visible mine patterns.

desc: Tiny (2mm) sphaerocerid with spotted frons (forehead). On decaying organic matter, dung, and compost. Part of the enormous family Sphaeroceridae — tiny detritivore flies that collectively play a major role in organic matter decomposition.
hp: Coprophagous — on dung. Nutrient recycler and livestock parasite suppressor.

desc: Medium bombyliid with diagnostic wing venation showing three discal cells (most bee flies have two). On sandy ground near host burrows. Larvae parasitize moth pupae or solitary bee nests. Distinguished from similar dark bee flies by wing cell count.
hp: Bee fly — larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees. Parasitoid.

desc: Medium bee fly with variable wing coloring. Parasitoid of various solitary Hymenoptera. Hovers near host burrows.
hp: Bee fly — larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees. Solitary. Parasitoid.

desc: Tiny bee fly. Larvae likely parasitize solitary bees. Part of the diverse western bombyliid fauna.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Parasitoid. Very small species.

desc: Tiny fly inducing bud galls on goldenbush (Isocoma). Native gall-maker on native shrub.
hp: Plant gall-inducer. Each species forms unique gall structures. Tiny. Tiny fly inducing bud galls on goldenbush (Isocoma).

desc: Medium therevid with warm brownish-tan body (fucata = painted/colored). Desert and foothill sandy habitats. Larvae predatory in sandy soil. Adults rest on sandy ground, well-camouflaged. Part of the diverse arid-land stiletto fly fauna.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Desert habitat.

desc: Medium bright yellow tachinid parasitoid. Unusual among tachinids for its vivid yellow coloring (most are gray or brown). Attacks stink bug nymphs — female lays eggs on host. Important biocontrol agent of pest pentatomids. On flowers when not hunting.
hp: Biocontrol agent; parasitoid. Parasitoid of stink bugs (Pentatomidae).

desc: Narrow fruit fly — small tephritid with banded wing pattern. Larvae develop in stems of various Asteraceae. Part of the huge tephritid radiation on composites — each composite plant genus supports its own suite of picture-wing fly species. On native and weedy composites.
hp: Larvae develop inside fruit. Crop pest. On Asteraceae (composites). Ant associate.

desc: Tiny fly of rocky intertidal splash zone. Larvae in algae and surf-washed debris. One of the few truly marine flies.
hp: Rocky shoreline habitat.

desc: Tiny ephydrid at water margins. Larvae in aquatic algal mats. Part of diverse shore fly fauna.
hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Tiny. Tiny ephydrid at water margins. Larvae in aquatic algal mats.

desc: Tiny cecidomyiid inducing galls on various native plants. Each gall species is host-specific. Part of the enormous gall midge family (6,000+ species worldwide).
hp: Plant gall-inducer. Each species forms unique gall structures. Tiny. Tiny cecidomyiid inducing galls on various native plants. Ea.

desc: Tiny agromyzid. Larvae mine in nettle leaves. Part of the diverse leaf-mining fly community.
hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Agromyzidae. Very small species. Fly. Agromyza.

desc: Small, beautiful bombyliid with dense golden pile and subtle wing banding. Visits flowers for nectar. Larvae parasitize ground-nesting solitary bees. Named 'zona' for the zoned wing pattern. One of the more attractive small bee flies in chaparral habitats.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Chaparral habitat. In chaparral habitat. Zoned.

desc: Large dark fly whose larvae parasitize rodents. Huge larvae grow under host skin. Adults rarely seen.
hp: Parasitoid — larvae develop inside insect host. Natural pest control.

desc: Small dark tachinid. Parasitoid of caterpillars. Part of the important tachinid parasitoid guild.
hp: Predator or parasitoid of caterpillars — natural pest control. Preys on caterpillar. Caterpillar predator.

desc: Canyon sunflower leafminer — tiny agromyzid making serpentine mines specifically in canyon sunflower (Venegasia dentata) leaves. Host-specific — a native fly on a native plant. The winding mine pattern in the large leaves is easily visible. An evolved specialist.
hp: On sunflowers/composites (asteraceae). Leaf miner.

desc: Tiny agromyzid whose larvae create blotch mines in leaves of various plants. Each mine pattern is species-diagnostic — experts can identify the fly from the mine shape alone. Part of the species-rich leaf-mining fly community on LA's native and ornamental plants.
hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Agromyzidae. Very small species. Fly. Nemorimyza.

desc: Spurred robber fly — medium asilid with diagnostic spurred hind legs (calcaneus = heel spur). Perches on ground and low rocks in open habitats, darting out to intercept small flying insects. Desert and foothill scrub. Part of the diverse ground-perching robber fly guild.
hp: Robber fly — aerial predator catching other insects in flight. Desert specialist.

desc: Large hairy robber fly with dense golden pile. Bumblebee mimic. Named for dipterist D.W. Coquillett.
hp: Bumblebee-mimicking robber fly — dense golden pile. Aerial predator of large insects.

desc: Tiny fly inducing blister galls on saltbush (Atriplex) leaves. Common in coastal and desert scrub.
hp: Plant gall-inducer — larvae develop inside species-specific gall tissue. Desert specialist.

desc: Metallic blue blow fly with distinctive leg ornamentation. On carrion and flowers. Forensically important.
hp: On carrion — essential decomposer. Forensically important. Calliphoridae.

desc: Non-biting chironomid. Larvae aquatic in various freshwater habitats — ponds, streams, lakes. Important fish food — chironomid larvae (bloodworms) are the dominant invertebrates in many freshwater systems. Adults form mating swarms. Completely harmless.
hp: Family Chironomidae.

desc: Medium conopid parasitizing bees. Female intercepts bees in flight to lay egg. Wasp-like.
hp: Internal parasitoid of bees — develops inside host. Family Conopidae. Medium conopid parasitizing bees. Female intercepts bees in.

desc: Small dark bibionid swarming in spring. Sluggish swarms. Larvae decompose soil organic matter.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down organic matter. Preys on slug. Soil-dwelling.

desc: Small gray robber fly of sandy habitats. Sits on sand, darting up to catch passing insects.
hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. Asilidae.

desc: Native stratiomyid related to the commercially important black soldier fly (H. illucens). Larvae decompose organic matter. Part of the growing interest in soldier fly biology for waste management — relatives are commercially farmed for protein production.
hp: Wood decomposer — breaks down dead wood.

desc: Flat-faced bee fly — medium bombyliid with characteristically flattened face. Desert habitats on sandy ground. Larvae parasitize solitary ground-nesting bees and wasps. Adults hover near ground seeking host burrows. The flattened face is unusual among bee flies.
hp: Burrowing ambush predator. Desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: Large hover fly mimicking a spider wasp. Dark with metallic sheen. On flowers. Impressive wasp mimic.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Wasp mimic. Metallic coloring.

desc: Tiny (2-3mm) yellowish fly on vegetation in gardens. Part of the poorly known family Chyromyidae — small yellow flies whose biology remains largely unstudied. Likely feeds on plant exudates or microorganisms on leaf surfaces. One of LA's most obscure flies.
hp: Garden-associated fly — larvae or adults in garden habitats.

desc: Small tephritid with single dark band across wings. On various plants. Larvae in fruit or stems. Distinguished from similar tephritids by the single (not multiple) wing band. Part of the enormous picture-wing fly diversity.

fm.Color: Pale with single dark wing band

hp: Larvae develop inside fruit. Crop pest.

desc: Small fuzzy bombyliid of sandy desert ground. Named 'transitus' for the transitional position it occupies between hovering and perching behavior. Larvae parasitize ground-nesting bees and wasps. On sandy washes where host nests occur.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Arizona Ash. Desert habitat.

desc: Ornate-collared fungus gnat — small dark mycetophilid with diagnostic ornate thorax markings. Larvae on fungi in woodland litter. Adults at lights near forest. Named for the decorative thoracic pattern. Part of the vast but poorly known fungus gnat community.
hp: Mycetophagous — feeds on fungi and fungal hyphae. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org. Forest. Forest habitat.

desc: Schiner's poplar twiggall fly — tiny agromyzid inducing diagnostic swollen galls on poplar (Populus) twigs. Each gall is a section of twig that swells to 2-3x normal diameter. Host-specific — only on poplars and cottonwoods. Named for Austrian dipterist I.R. Schiner.
hp: On cottonwood/poplar (populus) in riparian habitat. Agromyzidae.

desc: Tiny cecidomyiid named for Samuel Wendell Williston, pioneering American dipterist who wrote the first comprehensive Manual of North American Diptera (1908). Induces galls on various native plants. Part of the hyperdiverse gall midge family.
hp: Plant gall-inducer. Each species forms unique gall structures.

desc: Blue catkin fly — elongated hover fly with metallic blue-green body and long face. Larvae develop in wet tree holes and sap runs. Adults visit catkins and tree wounds for sap. Part of the sap-run specialist syrphid community. The metallic blue coloring is unusual for a hover fly.

fm.Color: Dark; long face; at sap runs

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Syrphidae.

desc: Tiny cecidomyiid inducing galls on California sagebrush (Artemisia) that exude sticky resinous substance. The sticky surface may protect the developing larva from parasitoid wasps. Part of the remarkable Rhopalomyia radiation on sagebrush.
hp: Parasitoid — biological control agent. Associated with California Sagebrush.

desc: Fleabane bud gall midge — tiny cecidomyiid inducing rosette-like bud galls on fleabane (Erigeron). Host-specific — each gall midge species typically attacks only one plant genus. Galled buds fail to open, forming dense rosettes. Very common on roadside Erigeron.
hp: On Rosa (roses) — garden and wild species. Tiny

desc: Medium therevid with pointed abdomen. Named for early California entomologist C.F. Baker. Larvae predatory in sandy soil — hunt other soil invertebrate larvae. Adults on sandy ground and low vegetation. Part of the diverse desert stiletto fly fauna.
hp: Soil predator — hunts in leaf litter. Desert habitat.

desc: Medium conopid with diagnostic angled dark wing band. Internal parasitoid of bees and wasps — female intercepts host in flight, inserts egg between abdominal segments. Larva develops inside living host. Wasp-like appearance. On flowers.

fm.Color: Brown with oblique wing band; parasitizes bees

hp: Family Conopidae.

desc: Small delicate crane fly of moist woodland and riparian habitats. Larvae in wet soil and moss. Adults weak fliers. Named 'dulcis' (sweet). Part of the species-rich limoniid crane fly family — distinguishing species requires wing venation and male genitalia.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Riparian habitat.

desc: Tiny cecidomyiid inducing distinctive galls on monkeyflower (Diplacus/Mimulus) flowers and stems. Host-specific. Each gall houses a single developing larva. Part of the enormous Cecidomyiidae — the most species-rich fly family, with thousands of species.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. In buildings. Gall-inducer.

desc: Medium bee fly named for dipterist C.W. Johnson. On flowers in desert and foothill habitats.
hp: Ambushes prey on flowers. Desert habitat. Family Bombyliidae. Johnson's.

desc: Small dark mycetophilid whose larvae feed on bracket fungi in woodland. Part of the enormous but poorly known fungus gnat community — thousands of species decompose fungi, forming a crucial link between fungal and arthropod food webs.
hp: Decomposer; fungi; fungus. Fungal associate — see lafungi.org.

desc: Non-biting chironomid with darkened tibiae. Larvae aquatic — an important component of the freshwater benthic community. Fish food. Adults in mating swarms near water. Part of the dominant aquatic insect family — chironomids outnumber all other aquatic insects.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Aquatic.

desc: Small fly with disproportionately large head. Larvae are internal parasites of leafhoppers. Important biocontrol.
hp: Pipunculid — tiny big-headed fly whose larvae parasitize leafhoppers (Cicadellidae) internally. Important biocontrol of leafhopper pests.
